_metadata.description: Explicit exoneration allowlist for SAME_UNII_DIFFERENT_NAMES audit findings. Each entry documents a UNII that maps to multiple reference entries which are legitimately the same FDA-cataloged compound (common vs Latin name, branded form vs generic, active vs inactive role, etc.). Listed here, the audit emits 'review' severity instead of 'critical', and the Sprint 1 UNII-first matching path is safe to land because runtime priority order (banned > IQM > standardized > botanical > other) resolves correctly.

field_contract.fda_canonical_name: Canonical name from fda_unii_cache.json::unii_to_name — the FDA-authoritative substance name
field_contract.entries: List of {file, entry_id} that share this UNII
field_contract.rationale: Plain-English explanation of why these entries legitimately share the same FDA UNII

_metadata.audit_rule: Per pre-Sprint-1 blocker rule (docs/UNII_TRIAGE_2026_05_14.md): no SAME_UNII_DIFFERENT_NAMES critical finding may remain in the audit's post-exoneration output unless it is in this allowlist with rationale + FDA canonical name + regression test (scripts/tests/test_unii_exoneration_allowlist.py).

exonerations.rationale: Both are branded mannitol excipients (Parteck = Merck, Pearlitol = Roquette). Same FDA-cataloged compound, different brand names. Single tier (other_ingredients), so runtime collision logging will warn but the resolved payload is correct either way.
Common name (Winged Treebine) vs Latin binomial (Cissus quadrangularis) for the same plant. Standardized entry wins for active-side scoring.

All maritime pine (Pinus pinaster) bark extract. Pycnogenol is the Horphag-branded standardized form. IQM wins for active scoring. 2026-05-22: pine_bark_extract relocated from standardized_botanicals to botanical_ingredients (plain identity, no bonus) per MO-3; std-side pycnogenol retains the branded bonus pathway.

All derived from FDA-cataloged Citrus aurantium dulcis fruit powder. Crystals and flavor are downstream products of the same orange fruit. Botanical wins for scoring; other entries route to inactive recognition.
Common Chinese name (Danshen) vs Latin binomial (Salvia miltiorrhiza) for the same plant. Standardized entry wins for active scoring.
Same compound (DMG, N,N-dimethylglycine), IQM is active-scoring entry, other_ingredients is inactive-recognition entry. Different roles, identical chemistry. IQM wins at runtime.
Latin binomial (Kaempferia parviflora) vs common name (Black Ginger) for the same plant. IQM wins.

All Zingiber officinale (ginger) entries. Intentional separate modeling for scoring context: raw root vs standardized extract vs flavor preparation. Priority order resolves to IQM for active scoring; other tiers route inactive disclosures.
Same compound (CLA), two roles. IQM is the active-scoring entry; other_ingredients is the inactive-recognition entry for CLA oil used as a carrier. IQM wins.
Same spice (Syzygium aromaticum / Eugenia caryophyllus), two contexts (whole/herb vs powdered formulation aid). Botanical wins for scoring.
Latin binomial (Isatis tinctoria) vs common name (Woad) for the same plant. IQM wins.
Himematsutake is a common Japanese name for Agaricus blazei. Both entries point to the same FDA-cataloged whole botanical; standardized botanical wins when active-scoring context needs it.

AstaZine is a branded Haematococcus pluvialis astaxanthin source. Both standardized-botanical entries intentionally share the same FDA substance; first-write routing remains within the same identity family.

Sharp-PS Green is a branded phosphatidylserine ingredient, while IQM and botanical entries model the same FDA-cataloged phosphatidylserine identity in different scoring contexts. IQM wins for active scoring.
Both rows model annatto colorant/extract preparations under the same FDA annatto substance. The broad variant row and extract row are inactive-recognition aliases, not separate chemical identities.
Levagen is a branded palmitoylethanolamide ingredient. The IQM parent and branded standardized entry share the same FDA palmitamide MEA identity; IQM remains the active-scoring winner.
Lemon bioflavonoids are a citrus-bioflavonoid source row, while IQM models the active scoring parent. Both carry the FDA citrus bioflavonoids substance and route to the IQM parent for active scoring.
Goat's rue is the common name for Galega officinalis. These botanical rows are aliases for the same FDA-cataloged whole plant, not competing identities.
The IQM parent, plural botanical row, and standardized elderberry row all represent European elderberry. IQM wins for active scoring; botanical and standardized rows keep source/form context.
Glycerol monooleate and glyceryl monooleate are naming variants for the same FDA-cataloged excipient. Both rows are inactive-recognition aliases in the same other-ingredient tier.
Pau d'Arco and Pau d'Arco bark both point to the FDA-cataloged Handroanthus impetiginosus whole botanical. IQM wins for active scoring; the bark row preserves botanical label context.
Cacao powder is a cocoa-source botanical label row. Both records share the FDA cocoa identity, and the IQM parent remains the active-scoring payload.
The IQM form-level row and botanical row both represent hesperidin. Form-level UNII lookup still routes to the citrus bioflavonoids parent payload while retaining form identity downstream.
PVP, povidone, and Kollidon are the same FDA-cataloged polyvinylpyrrolidone excipient identity represented with generic and branded labels. All rows live in the inactive other-ingredient tier.

CoffeeBerry is a branded whole coffee fruit extract, while IQM and botanical rows model the same Coffea arabica fruit identity. IQM wins for active scoring and the branded row preserves standardized-source context.
Cellulose gum is the common excipient label for sodium carboxymethylcellulose. Both rows represent the same FDA-cataloged inactive ingredient identity.

Chromax is a branded chromium picolinate ingredient. The IQM form-level row and standardized branded row share the same FDA chromium picolinate identity, while the IQM parent remains the scoring payload.
Cutch tree is the common name for Senegalia/Acacia catechu. Both records represent the same FDA-cataloged whole botanical; IQM wins for active scoring.
FloraGLO and Lutemax 2020 are branded lutein ingredients. All rows share the FDA lutein identity, with IQM providing the active-scoring parent and branded rows preserving formulation/source context.
Live GSRS search for partially hydrolyzed guar gum resolves to the FDA guar gum substance. The plain guar gum and PHGG rows are inactive/fiber recognition variants of the same registry identity.

Both rows model sodium starch glycolate type A disintegrant/excipient labels. Live GSRS search resolves the ingredient name to the same FDA mixture UNII, so this is duplicate inactive modeling rather than a competing identity.
The calcium chloride processing-aid row and excipient row intentionally represent the same FDA calcium chloride chemical. The duplicate rows preserve different inactive-use contexts.

The IQM parent, botanical row, and standardized botanical row all represent Hypericum perforatum / St. John's Wort. IQM wins active scoring while botanical and standardized rows preserve source and marker context.

Vitamin C IS ascorbic acid (same compound). Two roles: IQM models it as an active vitamin; other_ingredients models it as a preservative additive. IQM wins for active scoring; other tier handles inactive disclosure as a preservative.
